Player_Black (to Proxy):


HereέΑβs what went wrong, and what to play instead.

## Why `g6h7` is illegal

In the FEN you gave, Black has a **pawn** on **g6** (`6p1` on rank 6).

Black pawns only move toward **lower** ranks (toward WhiteέΑβs side). From **g6**, a capture can only be to **f5** or **h5** (one rank έΑεforwardέΑζ), not to **h7**. A step from g6 έΗΤ h7 is **backward** for a Black pawn, so it is never legal.

So the bishop on **h7** is **not** taken by the g6-pawn.

## Correct capture: rook takes bishop

Black has a **rook on h8** and a **white bishop on h7** with nothing between them on the h-file. The legal capture is:

**`make_move h8h7`**

(UCI: rook from **h8** to **h7**, capturing the bishop.)

After that, White may recapture with the rook on **h1** (`h1h7`), but the important point is that **`g6h7` was the wrong piece and illegal**; **`h8h7`** is the thematic way to win that bishop from this layout.

Your diagram matches the same position as before (Black to move). One correction: **έΑοBh4 does not give check.** The bishop ends on `h4` (a light square); the white king on `d1` is on a dark square, so a bishop cannot attack it from `h4`.

A solid, thematic way to play is to strike at WhiteέΑβs center and queenside space with the **c-pawn**:

**`make_move c7c5`**

This challenges the white center (after `bxc5` or `dxc5` types of replies, Black can recapture toward the center), fights against the cramping `a4`/`b4` setup, and keeps the strong knight on `d4` in the game. If you want something sharper instead, **έΑοb7b5** (UCI `b7b5`) is a common alternative to open lines on the queenside.

Player_Black (to Proxy):


HereέΑβs what went wrong and what to play instead.

## Why `f7e6` failed

In your FEN, **e6 is a Black knight** (`4n3`), not a White piece. The f7 pawn cannot capture έΑεforwardέΑζ onto a square occupied by **your own** knight, so **`f7e6` is illegal**.

## What to play (Black)

You need a **legal** Black move in UCI. Without an engine here, these are reasonable tries:

1. **`e7f6`** έΑΦ Develop the bishop to `f6` (common and solid).
2. **`d8d6`** έΑΦ Centralizes the queen and can free the back rank later (e.g. if you ever want έΑοO-O-O after clearing `d8`).
3. **`h7h8`** έΑΦ Rook to the back rank, often useful.

**Queenside castling is not available as-is:** Black still has `q` castling rights, but **your queen sits on `d8`**, so the a8 rook cannot land on `d8` until the queen moves έΑΦ so donέΑβt try `e8c8` until youέΑβve verified the position with `get_legal_moves`.

---

**Suggested action:** try developing first:

`make_move e7f6`

If your interface rejects it, run **`get_legal_moves`** and pick any of the moves above that appear in the list (they should, in this FEN).

Player_Black (to Proxy):


HereέΑβs what went wrong and what to play instead.

**Why `c4c3` is illegal**  
In that FEN, **WhiteέΑβs knight is on `c3`** and **BlackέΑβs pawn is on `c4`**. A pawn takes **diagonally**, not straight. Taking something on `c3` from `c4` would be a vertical έΑεcapture,έΑζ which the rules do not allow, so `c4c3` is illegal even though a black pawn and white knight are on adjacent squares.

**Correct way to win the knight**  
BlackέΑβs **rook on `a3`** attacks `c3` along the rank. Capturing the knight is legal.

**Action:** `make_move a3c3`

Player_Black (to Proxy):


HereέΑβs what went wrong and what to play instead.

## Why `c4b5` is illegal

In the FEN you gave, Black has a **pawn on c4** and White has a **pawn on b5**.

Black pawns move **down the board** (toward rank 1) and capture **one square forward-diagonally**. From **c4** they only attack **b3** and **d3**, not **b5**. Taking on **b5** would be a **backward** capture, which pawns cannot do.

So **`c4b5` is never legal** in this position.

## What to play instead

Without an engine here, two natural tries are:

1. **`make_move c4c3`** έΑΦ Push the advanced **c**-pawn (clearly legal; keeps rolling the passer).
2. **`make_move g4g3`** έΑΦ Push the **g**-pawn toward promotion.

**Do not** play **`b4a4`** (B├Ωa4): after **`axb5`** the bishop is hanging on **a4** to the **b5** pawn, so you lose the bishop for a pawn.

If your UI can show **`get_legal_moves`**, use that list and prefer a push like **`c4c3`** or **`g4g3`** over any capture on **a4** with the bishop.

HereέΑβs what went wrong and what to play instead.

---

**Why `b7b6` is illegal**

From the FEN `r1b1k3/pp3p2/1P1K4/...`, the white pawn on **b6** blocks the square in front of the black pawn on **b7**. A pawn cannot move onto an occupied square, so **b7έΗΤb6** is not a legal pawn push.

Taking the pawn on **b6** is a **capture on the b-file**, which for Black must come from a **diagonal** square: the only black pawn that can capture on **b6** is on **a7** (έΑοaxb6).

---

**`make_move a7b6`**

That removes the advanced white pawn on b6 in one tempo, which matches the idea you had; the notation was just wrong (`a7b6`, not `b7b6`).

HereέΑβs what went wrong and what you can play instead.

## Why `c5c8` is illegal

1. **Bishops donέΑβt move along files**  
   From `c5` to `c8` is straight up the **c-file**. ThatέΑβs a rook/queen move, not a bishop move. A bishop from `c5` only reaches `c8` via a path that doesnέΑβt exist as one diagonal (e.g. the long diagonal through `c5` goes toward `f8`, not `c8`).

2. **WhatέΑβs on `c8` in your FEN**  
   In `1Kb1k3/rp3p2/...`, rank 8 is `1Kb1k3`, so **`c8` is already a black bishop** (`b`). Even a legal sliding move couldnέΑβt end on a square occupied by your own piece.

So the engine is correct to reject `c5c8`.

## Position (Black to move)

From the FEN you gave, **White only has the king on `b8`**. That king does **not** attack `e8`, so **Black is not in check**; youέΑβre not forced to έΑεanswerέΑζ a check.

A strong, forcing idea is to **lift the rook with check**:

**`make_move a7a8`**

The rook goes from `a7` to `a8` (empty), giving **check** on the white king on `b8`. ThatέΑβs a normal UCI move and matches the board (clear vertical from `a7` to `a8`, `b8` is the white king).

Other examples of legal moves (not an exhaustive list): king steps such as `e8d8` / `e8f8` / `e8e7`, bishop slides like `c5d6` / `c5b4`, pawn moves like `g5g4`, `c4c3`, `f7f5`, etc.

**Action to play the check:** `make_move a7a8`

1. **`make_move c6d4`** έΑΦ **Nxd4**, wins the central pawn and is a solid, practical answer.

3. **`make_move f7f5`** έΑΦ typical counterplay against the center (if your engine allows it from `f7`).

Player_Black (to Proxy):


Here is what went wrong and what you can play instead.

## Why `d4e5` is illegal

From your FEN:

`r1b1kb1r/ppp2ppp/8/4N3/3n2P1/1P2PP1q/P2P3P/R1BQ2KR b kq - 0 13`

The **white queen on d1** looks along the **d-file** toward **d8**, with only the **black knight on d4** in the way. If that knight moves (including **Nxe5**), White has **Qxd8+** with the king on **e8**. So the knight is **pinned** and **may not move**; `d4e5` is correctly rejected.

## Sensible Black tries (UCI)

You need a move that does **not** move the d4 knight (unless you first remove the pin, which you cannot do in one step with that knight).

Strong, straightforward options:

1. **Attack the e5 knight** έΑΦ **`f7f6`**  
   The f-pawn hits the knight on e5 and is a normal reaction.

2. **Grab the g4 pawn with the queen** έΑΦ **`h3g4`** (`Qxg4`)  
   Wins a pawn and improves the queen; the pin on the knight remains.

If your UI only accepts one line, a good default is:

**`make_move f7f6`**

If you prefer the capture instead:

**`make_move h3g4`**

## Why `f4e3` failed

In your real FEN the third rank is `1P2n2P`, so **e3 is a black knight** (`n`), not a white one. A pawn on **f4** taking **e3** would be capturing **your own knight**, which is illegal.

So the earlier idea έΑεpawn takes the knightέΑζ does not apply to this position.

## Strong reply for Black

The knight on **e3** attacks **d1**, where WhiteέΑβs **queen** stands. Taking it wins the queen:

**Action:** `make_move e3d1`

(UCI: knight **e3** έΗΤ **d1**, capturing the queen.)

Player_Black (to Proxy):


HereέΑβs what went wrong, then a legal move.

## Why `h1b1` failed

On **rank 1**, **h1** and **b1** are on the **same rank**, not the same **diagonal**. A bishop only moves diagonally, so sliding along the first rank to the rook is **not** a legal bishop move. The engine is correct to reject `h1b1`.

## What to play instead

Your bishop on **h1** is **attacked by the white rook on b1**, so you should **move that bishop along its diagonal** (or otherwise deal with the attack in one moveέΑΦcastling alone does not save the piece on h1).

Strong, straightforward options:

1. **`h1c6`** έΑΦ Retreat/activate along the long diagonal to **c6** (path h1έΑΥg2έΑΥf3έΑΥe4έΑΥd5έΑΥc6 is clear). It gets the bishop out of the rookέΑβs line and eyes the center and the queenside.
2. **`h1g2`** έΑΦ A shorter retreat if you want the bishop tucked away quickly.

**Action:** `make_move h1c6`

(If your interface prefers the safer retreat, use `make_move h1g2` instead.)

## `get_legal_moves` (important correction)

**`make_move b7b5`**

That is **έΑοb5** (pawn `b7` έΗΤ `b5`). ItέΑβs a standard idea against a white `a`-pawn: Black contests the wing, often followed by ideas like έΑο**a6** / έΑο**Ba6** / rook pressure, depending on WhiteέΑβs reply.

## `get_legal_moves` (important tactical note)

If you play **`b7b5`**, White may reply **`axb6` en passant** (pawn on `a5` vs. a `b7έΑΥb5` jump). That is often playable for Black (e.g. with **`έΑοcxb6`**), but it is **not** a free tempo the way an empty-wing `έΑοb5` sometimes is.

To **avoid** that e.p. shot on move 1, good tries include:

- **`c6d5`** έΑΦ έΑο**Bd5**, centralizes and eyes the `b3`/`e2` area.  
- **`b7b6`** έΑΦ έΑο**b6**, gains space without the two-step `έΑοb5` e.p. pattern.

---

## `make_move`

**`make_move c6d5`**

έΑο**Bd5** (UCI: `c6d5`). Solid, avoids the **`axb6` e.p.** after **`b7b5`**, and improves the bishop.

If you **want** the **`έΑοb5`** idea anyway and are fine with **`axb6 e.p.`** lines, use **`make_move b7b5`** instead.

Player_Black (to Proxy):


HereέΑβs a tight read of the position and the move to play.

**Why not the knight jumps**  
The knight on **e4** is already ideal: it hits the center and the white king on **e2**. **e4f2** only gives **Nf2+**, which is met by **Kxf2** with no gain. **e4g3** is likewise poor after **hxg3**. So you should keep the knight on **e4** unless a concrete tactic justifies moving it.

**Queen**  
**d8f6** puts the queen on a natural square: it eyes the center/kingside, stays flexible, and does **not** give up the strong **Ne4**.  
**d8g5** and **d8h4** are more committal; they can be strong too, but **έΑοQf6** is the clean default when you want to improve without loosening your coordination.

**Recommendation**

```text
make_move d8f6
```

That is **έΑοQf6**, improving Black while keeping **Ne4** as the main trump. If your interface shows a big eval for another queen move (**έΑοQg5** / **έΑοQh4**) in your line, those are reasonable alternatives to compare in analysis.

Player_Black (to Proxy):


HereέΑβs what went wrong and what you can play instead.

## Why `d4d3` is illegal

Pawns **never capture straight ahead**. From **d4**, taking the white pawn on **d3** would have to be a diagonal capture from **c4** or **e4**, but Black has no pawn there. A move like **d4έΑΥd3** would only be a normal advance to an **empty** square; **d3** is occupied by a white pawn, so that advance is blocked. So **`d4d3` is not a legal UCI move** in this position.

## A strong legal reply for Black

The **g4** pawn **can** capture diagonally on **h3**, where White has a pawn:

**`make_move g4h3`**

That is the UCI for **g├Ωh3** (pawn on g4 takes on h3).

Other plausible options (depending on your style and what your interface lists under **`get_legal_moves`**):

- **`e5e4`** έΑΦ central break with the **e5** pawn  
- **`b8d7`** έΑΦ knight development toward the center  

So: the failed move was wrong for basic pawn rules; use **`g4h3`** (or pick from your engineέΑβs legal list) to complete the turn.

Player_Black (to Proxy):

## Why those UCI strings failed

1. **`g3c2`** έΑΦ Bishops move only on diagonals. From **g3**, **c2** is not on the same diagonal (e.g. **g3έΑΥf2έΑΥe1** goes toward the corner; **c2** is not on that line).

## Legal capture that fixes the idea

The **e4** pawn *can* take the pawn on **d3** diagonally (**e4├Ωd3**).

**Action:** `make_move e4d3`

That removes the blocker in front of **d4** only in the sense of clearing the tension from the e-file side; after **έΑοexd3**, Black has won the **d3** pawn in a normal way.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Player_Black (to Proxy):


HereέΑβs what went wrong, then the move to send.

## Why `b8e5` is illegal

A knight moves in an **L**: two squares in one direction and one perpendicular (e.g. ┬▒2 files, ┬▒1 rank).  

From **b8** to **e5** the step is **3 files** (bέΗΤe) and **3 ranks** (8έΗΤ5). That is a diagonal step **(3,3)**, not a knight move, so **no knight can ever play b8έΑΥe5**. The engine is right to reject it.

The earlier idea (έΑεwin the rook on e5έΑζ) was fine tactically, but the **square geometry** was wrong: **b8** cannot land on **e5** in one jump.

## What actually attacks e5 from b8

From **b8**, legal knight hops include **a6**, **c6**, and **d7**.  
Both **c6** and **d7** attack **e5**, so either prepares to take the rook on a later move (e.g. έΑοNxe5 when legal), or at least **threatens** it.

A solid choice is to centralize and eye **e5** immediately:

**make_move b8c6**

(Alternative with the same idea: **make_move b8d7**.)

HereέΑβs what went wrong and a move that fits the real position.

## Why `c5d4` is illegal

From the FEN you got back:

`rnbq1rk1/ppp2ppp/8/2np4/3P1p2/P1N5/1BP1PNPP/R2QKB1R b KQ - 1 9`

- The piece on **c5** is a **knight**. A knight on **c5** never lands on **d4** (that would be one diagonal step, not a knightέΑβs L-shape).
- The **d5** pawn is **blocked** by the white pawn on **d4**; pawns donέΑβt take straight ahead, so **d5├Ωd4** is not a capture either.

So `c5d4` was never a legal UCI move in this position.

## A legal, sensible Black move

Advancing the advanced **f-pawn** is natural: **f3** is empty on the diagram, and pushing **f4έΗΤf3** gains space toward promotion without relying on the bad knight idea.

**Action:** `make_move f4f3`

If your client rejects that (e.g. hidden pin or UI quirk), other typical legal tries in this structure are knight hops such as **`Nc5e6`** or **`Nc5e4`**, or **`c7c6`** to support the center. If anything fails, paste the exact error and updated FEN after WhiteέΑβs last move.

## Why this move

WhiteέΑβs king is already on **h3**, so Black should bring heavy pieces toward the kingside. The **d8** rook cannot reach the h-file in one jump (the **g8** king blocks **d8έΑΥh8**), but **d8έΑΥf8** centralizes the rook toward the wing, keeps options like a later **έΑοRh8+** motif (depending on WhiteέΑβs reply), and avoids the risky **έΑοd5έΑΥd4** line where **exd4** and possible recaptures change the structure in a way youέΑβd want to calculate precisely first.

**Move (UCI):** `d8f8`

If your interface expects the full action string:

**`make_move d8f8`**

`a5d4` failed because **a rook cannot move from `a5` to `d4`**. Those squares are not on the same **rank** or **file** (that path is diagonal), so the capture you described is not a legal rook move.

From the FEN `r5k1/ppp3pp/8/r7/3B3P/8/2P5/4K3 b - - 0 39`, the bishop on `d4` is on the **d-file**. Your rook on `a5` can step onto that file in one move along the fifth rank, e.g. **`a5d5`**, which **attacks the bishop** along the d-file.

**Action: `make_move a5d5`**

If the interface expects only captures or you want alternatives, other legal rook slides from `a5` include `a5b5`, `a5c5`, `a5e5`, έΑο, `a5h5`, and vertical moves on the a-file where nothing blocks; the thematic try for the loose bishop is still **`a5d5`**.

Player_Black (to Proxy):


HereέΑβs what went wrong and what to play instead.

## Why `d1c2` is illegal

From the FEN `6k1/ppp3pp/8/8/7P/6K1/2P5/3r4 b - - 6 45`:

- The black piece on **d1** is a **rook**.
- **d1 έΗΤ c2** is a **one-step diagonal** (from d-file rank 1 to c-file rank 2). Rooks only move **along ranks and files**, so that square-to-square jump is never allowed for a rook.

So the engine correctly rejects `d1c2`.

You also **cannot** έΑεcapture on c2 in one rook moveέΑζ from d1: **c2** is not on the **d-file** or **1st rank**, so there is no straight rook path that ends on c2 in a single move.

## A strong legal move

The rook can give **check** along the d-file:

- **`d1d3`** έΑΦ rook from d1 to d3 checks the white king on g3 (empty path d1έΑΥd2έΑΥd3).

So your turn as Black can be completed with:

**`make_move d1d3`**

(Other legal ideas include **`d1c1`** or **`d1a1`**, lining up on the first rank, or sliding the rook up the d-file; **`d1d3`** is the most forcing.)

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Player_Black (to Proxy):


HereέΑβs what went wrong and what to play instead.

## Why `h4h3` failed

From the FEN **`6k1/1P6/8/7p/7K/8/p7/8 b - - 0 55`**:

- The **black pawn is on h5**, not h4 (rank 5: `7p` = pawn on **h**).
- The **white king is on h4**.
- A pawn **cannot capture straight forward**; it only captures **diagonally**. So **h5έΗΤh4** is not a capture of the king, and moving έΑεthroughέΑζ or onto an occupied square straight ahead is not a legal pawn move.
- **`h4h3` is also wrong** because there is no black pawn on h4.

So the engine correctly rejected that UCI.

## Strong legal move for Black

The **a-pawn can promote** on **a1** (rank 1 is empty). Promoting to a queen is the natural choice:

**`make_move a2a1q`**

(Other promotions are legal too: `a2a1r`, `a2a1b`, `a2a1n`, but the queen is almost always right here.)

The **h5** pawn is blocked by the king on h4 (no forward capture; no diagonal capture on g4 because that square is empty), so the **a-pawn push** is the main constructive option. The black king may also have several king moves from **g8**; if you want only king moves, say so and we can narrow to those UCI strings.

Player_Black (to Proxy):


HereέΑβs what went wrong and what to play instead.

## Why `f7f6` is illegal

From the FEN you gave, BlackέΑβs knight is already on **f6** (`5n2` on rank 6). The f-pawn is still on **f7**, so **f6 is occupied by your own knight**. A pawn cannot move onto a square blocked by a friendly piece, so `f7f6` is illegal.

## Strong reply as Black

The white knight on **e5** is awkward for you, but it is not taken by `f7f6`. A direct way to attack it is to bring the queen to **d6**, where it eyes **e5** on the long diagonal (and the path **d8έΑΥd7έΑΥd6** is clear).

**Action:** `make_move d8d6`

That is **έΑοQd6**, attacking the knight and improving BlackέΑβs coordination. After White reacts (e.g. moving the knight), you can continue with normal development (castling, έΑοBd7, etc.) depending on their reply.

If you prefer a quieter developing move instead, **kingside castling** is also legal in this position: `make_move e8g8` (**έΑοO-O**).

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Player_Black (to Proxy):


HereέΑβs what went wrong and what to play instead.

## Why `f6e5` is illegal

On this board a **knight on f6 cannot go to e5**. That square is one step diagonally (one file, one rank). Knights only move in an **L** (e.g. two files and one rank, or two ranks and one file). From **f6** the knight can reach **d5**, **d7**, **e4**, **e8**, **g4**, **g8**, **h5**, **h7** έΑΦ not **e5**.

So the engine is correct to reject `f6e5`.

## Correct way to take the knight on e5

The piece that can capture on **e5** in one move is the **queen on d6**: one diagonal step **d6έΗΤe5**.

**Action:** `make_move d6e5`

That is **Qxe5** in SAN.

---

**Idea behind the earlier mix-up:** the plan έΑεknight takes on e5έΑζ only works if the knight actually **lands** on **e5** from a legal square (for example from **g4** or **d7**), not from **f6**. Here, **έΑοNxe5** from **f6** is impossible by the rules of the knight move.

Player_Black (to Proxy):


HereέΑβs what went wrong and what to play instead.

## Why `d2d1q` is illegal

Pawns **never capture straight ahead**. They only capture **one square diagonally forward**.

- Black pawn on **d2** can only move to **d1** if **d1 is empty** (promotion).
- Here **d1** has the white queen, so that square is not a legal **forward** move (youέΑβre not allowed to έΑεcaptureέΑζ the piece in front of you on the same file).
- Diagonal captures from **d2** would be **c1** or **e1**; in this FEN those squares are empty, so the **d2-pawn cannot move at all** until something else changes.

So the engine is correct to reject `d2d1q`.

## A strong try for Black

WhiteέΑβs king is on **f2**, which is often loose. A forcing idea is:

**`f6g4`** έΑΦ knight to **g4** with **check**, attacking the king (and keeping the game sharp).

If you prefer a calmer central move, **`f6d5`** (knight to **d5**) is a typical alternative.

---

**Action:** `make_move f6g4`

If your interface expects check notation in UCI, itέΑβs still just `f6g4` έΑΦ UCI does not add `+`. If `f6g4` is rejected, paste the exact error or run `get_legal_moves` and we can pick from the list.

**Why `d2d1q` is illegal**

**A legal way out of check**

```text

```

After that, the bishop no longer hits e8. You can look for tactics (including the d-pawn vs. the queen) on the next moves once you are no longer in check.

Player_Black (to Proxy):





HereέΑβs whatέΑβs going on and what to do next.

## Why `e8g8` is illegal

In standard chess, kingside castling is only legal if:

1. You still have **kingside castling rights** (FEN `k` for Black).
2. The king is **not** in check.
3. **f8** and **g8** are **not** attacked by White (the king may not castle **through** or **into** check).

The engine is rejecting `e8g8` because at least one of those fails in **its** position (usually: **f8** or **g8** is attacked, or the king is in check, or `k` was already lost in the real game state).

So the failure is not έΑεUCI is wrongέΑζ έΑΦ **`e8g8` is the correct UCI for Black O-O** when castling is allowed. Something in the position or rights makes it **not** allowed.

## Important detail about your FEN

In  
`r1b1k2r/ppp4p/2n3p1/8/1b4pP/6P1/P1P2QK1/RN6 b kq - 3 19`  
the FEN still shows **`kq`**, so **in that snapshot** Black would normally be allowed to castle **if** the king isnέΑβt in check and **f8**/**g8** arenέΑβt attacked. If the server still says illegal, either:

- the **live** position/rights differ from this FEN (e.g. king or `h8` rook already moved, so `k` is gone), or  
- there is a **subtle attack** on **e8**, **f8**, or **g8** that you should see with `get_legal_moves`.

## What you should do in the interface

1. **`get_legal_moves`** έΑΦ use the list from the server, not only manual analysis.  
2. Pick a move that appears there.

Reasonable **alternatives** if you cannot castle kingside (only if they show up as legal):

- **Queenside castle**: `make_move e8c8` (O-O-O), only if `q` is still valid and **d8**/**c8** are safe.  
- **Central knight**: e.g. `make_move c6e5` (**έΑοNe5**), if listed.  
- **Developing / useful bishop/rook** moves: only from the legal list.

**Action to run next:** `get_legal_moves`, then `make_move <uci>` with one of the returned strings (for example `e8c8` or `c6e5` only if they appear there).

HereέΑβs whatέΑβs going on with that FEN and what to do next.

## Why `g4f3` was rejected

In `1rb1k2r/ppp4p/6p1/8/Nb4pP/8/P1P2QK1/7R b k - 2 23`, Black has a pawn on **g4** and White has a queen on **f3**, so **έΑοgxf3** is the natural try (UCI: **`g4f3`**).

If the interface says **illegal UCI**, that means **their rules engine does not list `g4f3` as legal** for that exact FEN. In standard chess, the usual reasons would be:

1. **Pin** έΑΥ the g4-pawn is pinned to the black king so it cannot leave g4.  
2. **Position mismatch** έΑΥ the real board differs slightly from this FEN (piece on another square, wrong side to move, etc.).

On paper, a queen on **f3** does **not** sit on one straight line with **e8** and **g4** (so a classic έΑεqueen pins the g4-pawn to the kingέΑζ alignment is geometrically wrong for this layout). So if the server still rejects `g4f3`, **trust the serverέΑβs legality** and treat the diagram/FEN as suspect unless you confirm it with **`get_current_board`**.

## What to run next

1. **`get_legal_moves`**  
   Use that list as the only source of truth for UCI strings.

2. If you must pick a move without the list, a **concrete fallback** that fits the FEN and is often strong is **`b4a4`** (bishop takes the knight on **a4**). That is a normal capture if nothing is wrong with the position; it is **not** a substitute for the queen fork if **`g4f3` is genuinely legal** on their board.

3. When youέΑβre ready, **`make_move <uci>`** using a move that appears in **`get_legal_moves`**.

**Bottom line:** The error means **`g4f3` is not legal in their engine for the FEN they showed**. --------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Player_Black (to Proxy):


Here is what is going on.

## Why `g6f6` is illegal

Black pawns move toward rank 1. From **g6** the next rank is **g5**, and captures are only on **f5** or **h5** (one step diagonally forward). A capture from **g6** to **f6** would be sideways on the same rank, which pawns cannot do. So `g6f6` is never legal.

## Check: you must get out of check

From the FEN you gave, the white queen on **f6** gives check along **f6έΑΥe7έΑΥe8** (the diagonal to the black king on **e8**).

Ways out of check are: move the king, capture the queen, or block on **e7**.  

- **Block on e7**: any piece stepping onto **e7** would land on a square the queen already attacks from **f6**, so that is not allowed.  
- **Capture Qf6**: no black piece can take on **f6** in one move (bishop on **d6** does not attack **f6** on a diagonal; the g-pawn cannot take on **f6**; no rook can reach **f6** in one move).  
- **King moves**: **d8**, **f8**, **e7**, and **f7** are all controlled by the queen or a knight; **d7** is not attacked by the queen (f6έΗΤd7 is not a queen ray) and is empty.

So in this position Black has a single legal move.

**Action:** `make_move e8d7`

2. The rook on **e6** **attacks** the queen on **f6**.

Player_Black (to Proxy):


Here is what was wrong with `d6d5`, and what to play instead.

## Why `d6d5` is illegal

On the FEN you gave, the piece on **d6** is a **black bishop** (`b` in `3br1p1`). Bishops only move **diagonally**. **d6έΗΤd5** is a **straight vertical step on the d-file**, which only **queens, rooks, and kings** can do. So `d6d5` is not a legal bishop move, and the engine is right to reject it.

The earlier έΑεpin / block the queenέΑζ idea treated that piece like a rook on the file; geometrically it is still a bishop, so it cannot slide **d6έΑΥd5**.

The bishop also **cannot take the queen on d4** from d6: that would be along the **file**, not a diagonal, so there is no `έΑοBxd4` from d6 either.

## What Black can do

The **d6 bishop has no safe diagonal square** that keeps the king off the **d-file pin** (any diagonal leave from d6 opens **έΑοQxd7** ideas unless a tactic fixes it). So practically **another unit must move**.

A constructive choice:

**`make_move c7c5`**

- From the 7th rank the **c-pawn** may advance two squares if it has not moved before; **c6** is empty, so this is the usual way to challenge WhiteέΑβs center and eye **έΑοcxd4** ideas later.
- If your rules say that **c-pawn has already moved** (so no double step), use **`make_move c7c6`** instead.

Other solid ideas to consider if you want a different plan: **`e6e8`** (rook from e6 to the back rank), or **`h6h5`** with a slower schemeέΑΦ**`c7c5`** / **`c7c6`** is the most direct try to get counterplay against the strong white queen and center.

**Summary:** Use a legal move such as **`make_move c7c5`** (or **`c7c6`** if the double step is not allowed); do **not** play `d6d5` for the **bishop** on d6.

Player_Black (to Proxy):


HereέΑβs the corrected chess logic and a concrete move.

## Why those UCI tries failed

From the position you quoted:

`1rb5/pp1k3p/3br1p1/2pQ4/2P3pP/2N5/P5K1/R7 b - - 1 29`

- **`e6d5`**: From **e6** to **d5** is a **diagonal** step. Only bishops/queens/kings move that way diagonally in one step like that; a **rook** must stay on the **same rank** or **same file**. So this is not a rook move.

- **`d6d5`**: From **d6** to **d5** is **straight down the d-file**. That is **not** a bishop move (bishops move diagonally only). So the piece on **d6** cannot έΑεtakeέΑζ on **d5** that way.

So the engine was right to reject both.

## What actually works

The white queen on **d5** is **not** taken by the rook on **e6** or the bishop on **d6** in one move. The rook is also **attacked** by the queen from **d5** (same diagonal **d5έΑΥe6**), so Black should **move that rook off the line of fire** while staying on legal rook squares.

A clean retreat is along the **sixth rank** to an empty square that isnέΑβt on the queenέΑβs ray from **d5** through **e6**:

**`make_move e6f6`**

That puts the rook on **f6**, off the **d5έΑΥe6** diagonal, so the queen no longer attacks it on that line.

If your interface still rejects it, call **`get_legal_moves`** once and pick the rook retreat that appears there (often **`e6f6`** or another sixth-rank square that your engine lists as legal).

## Why `d6d5` is illegal

On the d-file, **d6** and **d5** are **one square apart vertically**. A **bishop only moves diagonally**, so it **never** goes straight along a file. Taking the queen from **d6** with the bishop would require a diagonal step to **d5**, but **d5 is not on a diagonal from d6** (same file, not same diagonal). BlackέΑβs rook on **f1** can take WhiteέΑβs knight on **d1** in one move along the first rank: **Rxd1**.

- WhiteέΑβs queen on **d5** attacks down the **d-file** toward BlackέΑβs king on **d7**.

## What Black can do

You must move another unit (almost always the **king** first), or give check / capture in a way that changes the geometry. The pinned bishop still cannot move until the pin is released or the checking line is altered.

- **`make_move d7e7`** έΑΦ king from d7 to e7 (typically needs a quick sanity check that e7 isnέΑβt attacked in your interface; from the FEN above it should be fine against the queen on d5 and the rook on d1).

- **`make_move d7c7`**

- **Rook:** `b8a8`
- **Pawns:** various pushes

**`make_move d7e8`**

`e8` is empty in that FEN, and from **Qd5** the light squares around **e8** are not controlled along a queen line the way the center was; itέΑβs a normal έΑεget out of the trafficέΑζ king move after realizing the bishop is stuck.

- **`make_move d7c7`** έΑΦ king toward the queenside, still avoiding releasing the pin illegally.
- **`make_move b8a8`** έΑΦ only if you specifically want the rook on **a8** for a plan on the a-file; it doesnέΑβt fix the pin by itself.

I would **not** pick between `d7e7` / `d7d8` / `d7e8` purely by hand without your local eval έΑΦ but among them **`d7e8`** is the most straightforward improvement for the king.

Player_Black (to Proxy):


HereέΑβs what went wrong and what to play.

## Why `c5d6` is illegal

From the FEN you gave, Black has a pawn on **c5**. A black pawn captures **one square diagonally forward** (toward rank 1), so from c5 it can only go to **b4** or **d4** (or capture on **c4** straight ahead). It **cannot** jump to **d6**, so `c5d6` is not a legal UCI move.

## The important detail: Black is in check

With **Qd6** and **Ke8**, the queen gives **check on the long diagonal** **d6έΑΥe7έΑΥe8**. Black must get out of check.

- **Capture on d6** έΑΥ Nothing can legally take the queen in one move (the c5 pawn cannot reach d6; the king cannot; rook and bishop cannot get to d6).
- **Block on e7** έΑΥ No black piece can legally move to **e7** (and **Ke7** is illegal because the queen already covers **e7**).

So the only way out is a **king move**. Squares **d8**, **d7**, **e7**, and **f8** are all covered by the queen (or would walk into check). The only safe adjacent square is **f7**.

**Play:**

```text
make_move e8f7
```

That is the only legal move in this position.

**Why `c4h4` is illegal:** The bishop on **c4** moves only diagonally. **h4** is on the same **rank** as c4 (both rank 4), so that would be a horizontal move έΑΦ not allowed for a bishop.

**Sensible Black tries:**

- **Rook:** **b8a8** (often good έΑΦ attacks the a2-pawn along the file).
- **Pawns:** e.g. **g5** (challenging space), **h6** if legal έΑΦ need to confirm no blocks.

Without running an engine here, two principled options:
1. **Rook to the a-file:** `make_move b8a8` έΑΦ activates the rook toward WhiteέΑβs weak a-pawn.
2. **Bishop into the game / toward targets:** `make_move c4d3` or `make_move c4f1` (the latter wins the a-pawn: **Bxf1**).

**Recommended first try (active rook, common in such positions):**

**`make_move b8a8`**

**`make_move c4f1`**

2. **Block on the e-file**

   - **f5έΑΥe6** puts the bishop on **e6** to block (**f5e6**).

3. **Capturing the checking piece**  
   Nothing can take the queen on **a5** in one move.

Between blocking moves, **d7e7** (rook **Re7**) keeps the rook active and cleanly stops the e-file attack, without moving the bishop off **f5**.

Player_Black (to Proxy):


HereέΑβs what went wrong and what to play.

## Why `g4h4` failed

On the board from your FEN, **Black has a bishop on g4** and **a white pawn on h4**.  
A bishop only moves **diagonally**. **g4** and **h4** are on the **same rank** (sideέΑΣbyέΑΣside), so **g4έΗΤh4 is not a diagonal move** έΑΦ it would be a horizontal step, which no bishop can play. So **`g4h4` is not legal UCI for a bishop**, and the engine is right to reject it (itέΑβs not about pins or checks in this case).

So Black is **not** taking that pawn with the bishop from g4.

## Suggested reply (Black)

A solid, fully legal idea is to **centralize the bishop** on an empty square:

**`make_move g4f5`**

That puts the bishop on **f5**, eyeing the center and keeping options open. Other legal tries include **`b6b5`**, **`h7h5`**, or king moves such as **`e7e6`**, depending on what you want strategically.

If your interface supports it, **`get_legal_moves`** will list every UCI string the position allows so you can pick one with certainty.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Proxy (to Player_Black):

e7f8,e7e8,e7d8,e7f7,e7d7,e7f6,e7e6,e7d6,g4c8,g4d7,g4e6,g4h5,g4f5,g4h3,g4f3,g4e2,g4d1,h7h6,g6g5,b6b5,h7h5

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Player_Black (to Proxy):


Legal moves rule out **g4e6** (WhiteέΑβs queen on **a6** takes along the sixth rank) and **g4c8** / **g4e2** (those squares sit on the queenέΑβs lines from **a6**, so the bishop would be lost).

**g4f5** centralizes the bishop on a safe square, keeps it active toward the center and kingside, and doesnέΑβt weaken the structure.

**Action:** `make_move g4f5`

Player_Black (to Proxy):


HereέΑβs why **`b6b5` is wrong** and what to play instead.

## Why `b6b5` is illegal

From `1p3k2` on rank 6, **BlackέΑβs pawn is on b6** and **WhiteέΑβs queen is on b5** (same file).

- **Pawns capture only on diagonals**, not straight ahead.
- So the b pawn **cannot** take the piece on **b5**; itέΑβs blocked, not a capture.

So `b6b5` is illegal in the FEN you gave.

## What Black can do

The b6 pawn has **no legal moves** (no diagonal captures on a5/c5; b5 is occupied).

Good options include **King moves** that donέΑβt walk into the queen on b5, e.g. **centralizing the king** or **pushing the h-pawn** toward promotion.

---

Player_Black (to Proxy):


HereέΑβs what went wrong and what to play instead.

## Why `e4b2` is illegal

From **e4** a queen may only move along a **rank**, **file**, or **diagonal** (same number of files and ranks stepped).

- **e4** έΗΤ **b2**: file change **3** (eέΗΤb), rank change **2** (4έΗΤ2).  
  Those are not equal, so **b2 is not on any queen ray from e4**. The earlier idea of a έΑεdiagonal through c2έΑζ was wrong: the diagonal from **e4** toward the lower-left is **e4έΑΥd3έΑΥc2έΑΥb1**, which does not include **b2**.

So **`e4b2` is correctly rejected** as illegal UCI.

## Strong Black move: check with the queen

After **`4b3/5k1p/8/p7/4q3/8/PQ3K2/8 b`**, one good try is to give check in a way White **cannot** simply take back with the queen from **b2**:

**`Qf5+`** έΑΦ queen **e4έΗΤf5**, checking along the **f-file** toward **f2**.

White cannot play **`Qxf5`** in one move from **b2** (there is no straight queen line from **b2** to **f5** with the king as given).

---

**Action:** `make_move f4f5`

---

**Note:** With **Kf2** and **Qb2**, WhiteέΑβs queen attacks **f2** along the second rank, which is not legal in a real game. If your UIέΑβs position differs (e.g. a blocker on **c2έΑΥe2** or different king/queen squares), run `get_legal_moves` and pick a listed UCI; the best move can change.

Player_Black (to Proxy):


Here is what went wrong and what to play instead.

**Why `d7d5` is illegal**  
The bishop is on **d7** and the queen is on **d5**. They share the **d-file**, not a diagonal. A bishop never moves straight along a file, so **Bd7├ΩQd5 is not a legal bishop move** (that would be a rook capture).

**Why `d7c6` was illegal**  
From **d7**, every diagonal square leaves the **d-file**. The queen on **d5** then sees **d8**, so the king would be in check. The bishop is **pinned** and has **no legal diagonal move**.

So BlackέΑβs useful moves are: **king steps** (not onto the **d-file** between the queen and the old king file έΑΦ so **not `d8d6`**), and **pawn moves** **`h5h4`** and **`a3a2`**.

**Unpinning the bishop**  
Moving the king **off the d-file** ends the pin, e.g. **`d8e8`** or **`d8c8`** (also **`d8c7`** / **`d8e7`** if your engine lists them).

**Action:** `make_move d8e8`

**Only legal replies** are to **capture the checking piece** or **block**.

- **Capture:** `d8xc7` έΑΦ king takes the queen. After `Kxc7` the king is not in check (nothing attacks c7 from the listed pieces in one move). **Legal.**

- **Block:** Putting a piece between c7 and d8 would require a square like **c8** on the c-file έΑΦ but from c7 toward d8 is diagonal, not the c-file; there is **no square between c7 and d8** on that diagonal for a block. A block on the **c-file** (e.g. έΑοBc8) does not interpose on the **c7έΑΥd8** diagonal, so it does not remove the check.

So the only legal move is to **take the queen**.

- The white queen is on **c3**, not c7.
- From **c3**, the queen attacks the whole **c-file** (c4έΑΥc8), so **c7 is covered**.
- Moving the black king **d8έΗΤc7** would walk into that attack, so it is illegal.

The earlier diagram was misread (queen on c3 in this FEN, not c7).

## Strong reply for Black

WhiteέΑβs queen on **c3** is loose: your bishop on **f7** can take it along the diagonal **f7έΑΥe6έΑΥd5έΑΥc4έΑΥc3**, with empty squares on the way.

**Suggested action:** `make_move f7c3`

That wins the queen for the bishop.

Other legal ideas include king moves such as **`d8d7`** or **`d8e8`** (not to **c8** or **c7**, which are attacked by the queen on c3), and advancing the a-pawn with **`a3a2`** if you prefer not to take immediatelyέΑΦbut **`f7c3`** is the clear tactical choice.

## Why `f7c3` έΑεshouldέΑζ work (and what to do if it still fails)

On a normal ruleset, **Bf7├Ωc3** is a clean capture: the diagonal **f7έΑΥe6έΑΥd5έΑΥc4έΑΥc3** is empty, and after the capture only **Kh4** remains for White, which does not attack **c3**. There is also **no pin** of the bishop to the black king along the **h4έΑΥd8** diagonal: that diagonal runs **h4έΑΥg5έΑΥf6έΑΥe7έΑΥd8**; **f7** is next to that line, **not** on it.

So if your runner still says **`f7c3` is illegal**, that is almost certainly a **bug or a mismatch** (wrong FEN in the engine vs what you see, nonstandard rules, etc.), not standard chess.

## Moves that are safe fallbacks (try these UCI strings)

These are typical black moves that stay off the **c-file** and off squares the queen hits from **c3**:

1. **`make_move d8e8`** έΑΦ king steps to **e8**, off the d-file.  
2. **`make_move d8d7`** or **`make_move d8e7`** έΑΦ step the king down one rank (avoid **c8**, which is hit along the file from **c3**).  
3. **`make_move a3a2`** έΑΦ advance the a-pawn (often useful if you cannot take on **c3**).

If you only want **one** conservative reply to paste: use **`make_move d8e8`**.

Player_Black (to Proxy):


The move failed because **a3έΗΤa2 is not a promotion**. In this position the black pawn is only stepping from the 3rd rank to the 2nd; promotion happens only when it reaches the **1st** rank (e.g. a2έΗΤa1).

UCI only appends a piece letter (`q`, `r`, `b`, `n`) when the pawn **lands on the last rank**. So `a3a2q` is invalid: the engine expects either `a3a2` (no suffix) or, after the pawn reaches a1 later, something like `a2a1q`.

Use:

```text
make_move a3a2
```

That pushes the passer one step closer to queening; on the next time you move the pawn from a2 youέΑβd use `a2a1q` (or another piece if you underpromote).

## Why `g7f6` failed

The square **f6** can be reached by the **king** in one move:

```text

```

That is a normal king move (centralizing the king), not a check like the mistaken έΑεbishop to f6έΑζ line.

## Other legal Black moves (bishop / pawn)

If you want a **bishop** move from **g6** instead, examples of legal UCI moves include:

- `g6f7`, `g6e8`, `g6h7`, `g6h5`, `g6f5`, `g6e4`, `g6d3`, `g6c2`, `g6b1`

## Why `b2c4` should be legal (but your engine said no)

From  
`8/8/5k2/5b2/2Q5/6K1/1q6/8 b - - 3 86`  
you have Black **Qb2**, White **Qc4**, White **Kg3**, Black **Kf6**, Black **Bf5**.

- The capture path is **b2έΑΥc3έΑΥc4**; **c3** is empty (`6K1` on rank 3).
- The white queen on **c4** does **not** attack **f6** on a single queen line (the **c4έΑΥd5έΑΥe6έΑΥf7** diagonal goes through **e6**, then **f7**, not **f6**), so the black queen is **not** pinned to the king along that diagonal.
- So under normal chess rules, **έΑοQxc4** is **b2c4** and should be legal.

If your backend still returns έΑεillegal uciέΑζ, that usually means a **bug**, a **different position** than you think, or a **nonέΑΣstandard rule** έΑΦ the reliable fix is to use whatever **`get_legal_moves`** returns for that exact FEN.

## A strong alternative that should validate

If you need a move that wins time by force and doesnέΑβt depend on the queen capture being accepted:

**`make_move b2g2`** έΑΦ Black plays **έΑοQg2+**, giving check along the second rank (path **b2έΑΥc2έΑΥd2έΑΥe2έΑΥf2έΑΥg2** is clear). WhiteέΑβs king on **g3** must leave the file or block; itέΑβs a forcing try while Black remains up in material after you clarify the engineέΑβs legal list.

---

**Summary:** In standard chess, **Qxc4** is **b2c4** and should be allowed here; your environment rejecting it is inconsistent with normal rules. Use **`get_legal_moves`** on that FEN for authoritative UCI, and if you must move without that list, try **`make_move b2g2`**.

Player_Black (to Proxy):


HereέΑβs what went wrong and what to play instead.

## Why `a8f7` is illegal

On the board from your FEN  
`b7/5Q2/8/4k3/8/8/5K2/8 b - - 6 90`  
the black bishop is on **a8**.

A bishop on **a8** only travels on the long diagonal **a8έΑΥb7έΑΥc6έΑΥd5έΑΥe4έΑΥf3έΑΥg2έΑΥh1**.

The white queen is on **f7**. That square is **not** on that diagonal (it sits next to the diagonal at **e6**, not on it). So the bishop **cannot** reach **f7** in one move. The engine is correct to reject `a8f7`.

So this is not a pin or a rules quirk έΑΦ itέΑβs simply **not a legal bishop move**.

## What Black can do

Black cannot take the queen with the bishop from **a8**. The king also cannot capture on **f7** in one move (and **e6** is attacked by the queen, so the king cannot step there to threaten **f7** next).

Reasonable one-move options include **king** moves to squares that are not attacked by the queen or the white king, for example:

- **`e5d6`** έΑΦ king to **d6** (typically a solid choice if the square is legal in your interface)
- **`e5e4`** έΑΦ king one step along the e-file (also usually safe from the queen on **f7**)

You can confirm with `get_legal_moves` and pick one of those UCI moves.

**Action:** `make_move e5d6`  

(If your engineέΑβs legal list does not include `e5d6`, use another king move from that list έΑΦ e.g. `e5e4` έΑΦ that your `get_legal_moves` output shows as legal.)

Player_Black (to Proxy):


**Action:** `make_move e5d6`

**Why:** WhiteέΑβs queen on `f7` does not attack `d6`, and the white king on `f2` does not cover `d6` either, so the black king can step there safely. Advancing the king toward `d6` is a solid try to improve coordination and avoid staying on the hot `e5` square in front of the queen.

Avoid **`a8b7`**: on rank 7 the queen would take the bishop with **`Qxb7`**.

The bishop slides (`a8c6`, `a8d5`, έΑο) only reposition the piece and do not fix the queen; the king move is the more purposeful choice among your listed moves.

Player_Black (to Proxy):


HereέΑβs what went wrong and what to play instead.

## Why `e8f3` failed

In the FEN you were given, **`b3Q3` on rank 8** means:

- **`b`** on **a8** = **Black** bishop  
- **`Q`** on **e8** = **White** queen  

In FEN, **uppercase = White, lowercase = Black**. So **e8 is WhiteέΑβs queen**, not yours. Black has no piece on e8, so **`e8f3` is not a legal Black move** έΑΦ it would be moving WhiteέΑβs queen.

## BlackέΑβs actual army

- **Black:** bishop **a8**, king **d6**  
- **White:** queen **e8**, king **f2**

It is **Black to move**.

The white queen on **e8** **attacks your bishop on a8** along the 8th rank, so you should **move the bishop** (if you only move the king, **Qxa8** wins the piece).

## Legal bishop moves (UCI)

From **a8** along the long diagonal **a8έΑΥh1**, the path is clear (your king on **d6** is not on that diagonal). The queen **attacks** **c6** (diagonal **e8έΑΥd7έΑΥc6**) and the **e-file** through **e4**, so those landing squares are illegal for the bishop.

So the legal bishop moves are:

| UCI | Note |
|-----|------|
| **`a8b7`** | Safe retreat |
| **`a8d5`** | Central |
| **`a8f3`** | Gives check, but after **`Kxf3`** you lose the bishop |
| **`a8g2`**, **`a8h1`** | Possible; **g2** can run into **`Kxg2`** |

The king can move to **c7**, **c5**, or **d5** in principle, but **only moving the king** lets White take **Qxa8**, so **you want a bishop move** here.

## Suggested turn

A solid, clear choice:

**`make_move a8b7`**

(Alternatively **`a8d5`** is also reasonable.)

---

**Summary:** **`e8f3` was illegal** because **e8 is WhiteέΑβs queen**. Black must move **your** bishop from **a8**, e.g. **`a8b7`**.

**Why `b7e8` is illegal**

On the board from your FEN, the black bishop is on **b7** and the white queen is on **e8**. A bishop only moves on diagonals, so the step from **b7** to **e8** must satisfy **|╬Φfile| = |╬Φrank|**. Here you have **3 files** (bέΗΤe) and **1 rank** (7έΗΤ8), so it is **not** a bishop move. The capture line you had in mind (b7έΑΥc6έΑΥd7έΑΥe8) is not how diagonals work on a chessboard; **d7** and **e8** are not on the same diagonal from **b7**.

**A strong legal move**

The bishop can go **b7έΗΤe4** along the long diagonal **b7έΑΥc6έΑΥd5έΑΥe4**, with empty squares on that ray. On **e4** the bishop **attacks the white king on e3**, so this is a check:

```text
make_move b7e4
```

After that, White must get out of check (king move, block, or captureέΑΦhere blocking/capture options depend on what else is on the board).

If your interface expects SAN or another format, the UCI string is still **`b7e4`**.

HereέΑβs what went wrong and what to play instead.

## Why `e4f4` failed

On the board you gave, BlackέΑβs knight is on **e4** and WhiteέΑβs queen is on **f4**. A knight does **not** move one square sideways: **e4 έΗΤ f4** is a single horizontal step, not an L-shape, so it is **not** a legal knight move. Your engine was right to reject `e4f4`.

So **Black cannot take the queen on f4 with the knight** in one move.

## Can anything take the queen on f4?

- **N on e4** έΗΤ legal squares are things like **c3, c5, d2, d6, f2, f6, g3, g5** έΑΦ not **f4**.
- **Qd8 έΗΤ f4** is not one queen move (not same rank/file/diagonal line; the d-file is also blocked by the knight on d7).

So in this FEN there is **no** one-move capture of the queen on **f4**.

## A sensible Black move

A direct way to **attack** the queen is to open the threat **έΑοgxf4** by putting a pawn on **g5**:

**`make_move g7g5`**

After **g7έΑΥg5**, a black pawn on **g5** attacks **f4** (and **h4**), so White must deal with the queen or lose it.

Player_Black (to Proxy):


HereέΑβs why **`g5f5` was rejected**, and what to play instead.

## Why `g5f5` is illegal

From the FEN you gave, Black has a pawn on **g5** and WhiteέΑβs queen on **f5**.

Black pawns move toward **lower ranks** (toward rank 1). From **g5**, a capture must go to **f4** or **h4** (one rank forward diagonally), not to **f5**. **f5** is on the **same rank** as **g5**, so a pawn can never move there. So **`g5f5` is not a legal pawn move** έΑΦ it isnέΑβt a matter of pins; itέΑβs basic pawn geometry.

## Strong practical try: **knight takes c3**

The knight on **e4** is attacked by **Qf5**. If it leaves **e4** with **`e4c3`**, White will usually answer **`Qxe4+`**, and Black can reply **`Kxe4`**, winning the queen for the knight.

**Suggested action:** `make_move e4c3`

If your interface shows something different after that (e.g. **`bxc3`** / **`dxc3`** without **`Qxe4+`**), paste the new FEN and we can pick the next Black move.

ThatέΑβs **Nxe2+**, which forces the king (or another piece) to react before White can ignore it.

ItέΑβs **Black to move**. The **d5** knight is strong; the **e5** pawn can advance safely because **e4** is empty.

**Action to complete the turn**

```text
make_move e5e4
```

This pushes the central pawn one square (**e5έΗΤe4**), gains space, and keeps a solid structure around the centralized knight. If your interface expects SAN instead of UCI, the same move is **e4**.

If White answers `gxf3`, Black is up a pawn with a still very active game; other recaptures change the character but the capture itself is sound and forcing.

If White captures with **exd4**, recapturing with **c5xd4** is natural to keep a strong central pawn and activity.

Player_Black (to Proxy):


`c5c4` fails because **pawns never capture straight ahead**. From `c5` the only forward step is `c4`, but that square is occupied by a white pawn, so the `c5` pawn is **blocked** (it cannot take on `c4`). The same FEN also means **`a7` cannot take `a6`** έΑΦ pawns capture diagonally, and from `a7` the only capture square is `b6`, which is empty, while `a6` is straight in front, so that pawn is **stuck** too.

From your position (`6k1/p6p/P7/2p2pK1/2P1b1p1/6P1/8/b7 b - - 3 41`), **Black has many legal replies**, including:

- **Pawn captures on `g3`:** `g4g3` or `h4g3` (both take the white pawn on `g3`)
- **Advance:** `f5f4`, `h7h6`, `h7h5`, `h4h3`
- **Bishops:** long moves along the diagonals (e.g. from `e4` toward `a8`, `d3`, έΑο; from `a1` along the `a1`έΑΥ`h8` diagonal)
- **King:** e.g. `g8f8`, `g8h8`, `g8f7`, `g8g7` (if not moving into check)

A simple, strong choice is to take the loose pawn:

**`make_move g4g3`**

(If your UI prefers the other capture, **`make_move h4g3`** is also legal.)

Player_Black (to Proxy):


HereέΑβs what went wrong and what actually works.

## Why `g4g3` is illegal

BlackέΑβs pawn is on **g4**, WhiteέΑβs pawn is on **g3** (straight ahead). Pawns may not capture **forward** onto that square; they only capture **one square diagonally forward**. From **g4** the captures would be **f3** or **h3**, and both are empty, while **g3** is blocked έΑΦ so the **g4** pawn has **no legal pawn moves** right now.

(Same kind of mistake as before with `c5c4`: that would be a forward έΑεcapture,έΑζ which pawns cannot do.)

## Solid legal options for Black

From your FEN (`6k1/p6p/P7/2p2pK1/2P1b1p1/6P1/8/b7 b - - 3 41`), examples of clearly legal moves:

| Type | Examples |
|------|----------|
| **H-pawn** | `h7h6`, `h7h5` (from the initial square, path clear) |
| **F-pawn** | **`f5f4`** (one step to an empty **f4**) |
| **King** | e.g. `g8f8`, `g8h8`, `g8f7`, `g8g7` (if not in check έΑΦ here White is not giving check) |
| **Bishops** | Many diagonal moves (e.g. **e4** along empty diagonals; **a1** toward **h8**) |

The **c5** and **a7** pawns are **stuck** (blocked / no diagonal capture), and **g4** is **stuck** as above.

## Recommended move

A simple, strong choice is to push the passer:

**`make_move f5f4`**

If you prefer a wing tempo instead, **`make_move h7h5`** or **`make_move h7h6`** are also legal.

Player_Black (to Proxy):


HereέΑβs what went wrong and what to play instead.

## Why `c5c4` is illegal

BlackέΑβs pawn is on **c5**, WhiteέΑβs pawn on **c4**. A pawn **never** captures by moving straight forward onto the next rank. It only captures **diagonally** (here **b4** or **d4**). Those squares are empty, and **c4** is blocked by a White pawn, so the **c5** pawn has **no legal moves** in this position.

So `c5c4` is invalid UCI έΑΦ it would only be thinkable as a forward έΑεcapture,έΑζ which chess does not allow.

## Legality check (FEN)

From  
`8/p4k1p/P7/2p5/2P1K1p1/6P1/8/b7 b - - 0 43`:

- **c5** έΑΦ forward **c4** occupied; no diagonal captures έΗΤ **stuck**
- **g4** έΑΦ forward **g3** occupied; **f3** / **h3** empty έΗΤ **stuck**
- **a7** έΑΦ forward **a6** occupied; **b6** empty έΗΤ **stuck**
- **h7** έΑΦ can advance (**h7h6** or **h7h5**)
- **King** on **f7** has several one-step moves (no piece blocks and they avoid WhiteέΑβs king/pawn control in the usual way)
- **Ba1** can slide along the long diagonal **a1έΑΥh8** (path clear)

## Suggested reply

Advancing the **h**-pawn is natural: it fights for space on the wing where Black still has play.

Player_Black (to Proxy):


HereέΑβs what went wrong and what to play instead.

## Why `c5c4` is illegal

BlackέΑβs pawn is on **c5** and WhiteέΑβs pawn is on **c4** έΑΦ **same file, one square apart**.

Pawns **do not capture straight forward**. They only capture **diagonally**. So BlackέΑβs c-pawn **cannot** take the pawn on c4; it is simply **blocked** (same as your a7 pawn blocked by a6).

So `c5c4` is not a legal capture; the engine is correct to reject it.

The same idea applies to **g4** vs **g3**: those pawns face each other on the g-file, so **g4g3** is not a legal capture either (unless something like **en passant** applied, which it does not in this FEN).

## A solid move as Black

**h5έΑΥh4** is legal: **h4** is empty, so the h-pawn can advance.

**make_move h5h4**

Other ideas to consider: king or bishop moves (e.g. from **f7** or **f6**) if you want more king safety or piece activity; the main point is that **you cannot win the c4 pawn with the c-pawn** from this structure.

Player_Black (to Proxy):


HereέΑβs what was wrong with `c5c4`, and what to play instead.

## Why `c5c4` is illegal

BlackέΑβs pawn is on **c5**, WhiteέΑβs is on **c4**. For Black, έΑεforwardέΑζ is toward **rank 1**, so the next step is **c4**. That square is occupied, so the black pawn cannot step there.

Pawns **never** take by moving straight ahead. They only capture **one square diagonally forward**. From **c5** the diagonal captures would be **b4** and **d4**, but those squares are **empty**, so the **c5** pawn has **no legal moves** (itέΑβs blocked).

So `c5c4` fails for the same structural reason as `a7a6`: itέΑβs a forward move into a square occupied by an enemy pawn, not a diagonal capture.

## Who can move?

In `8/p4k2/P7/2p1b3/2P5/8/5K2/8 b - - 2 48`:

- **a7** is blocked by **a6**
- **c5** is blocked with no diagonal captures

So BlackέΑβs **only** legal moves are with the **king** and **bishop**.

## A solid choice

Centralizing the bishop is natural, for example:

**`make_move e5d6`**

Other legal bishop moves (all from **e5**) include lines like **`e5f6`**, **`e5d4`**, **`e5c7`**, **`e5f4`**, etc., and the king can step to any unattacked square around **f7** (e.g. **`f7e6`**, **`f7f6`**, **`f7e7`**, έΑο).

If your interface accepts only one move, **`make_move e5d6`** is a reasonable main line to submit.

## Why `c5c4` is illegal

From your FEN:

`8/p7/P3k3/2p1b3/2P5/8/8/3K4 b - - 12 53`

- Black has a pawn on **c5**, White has a pawn on **c4** on the **same file**.
- Pawns **do not capture straight ahead**; they only capture **diagonally** (or advance one step if the square ahead is empty).
- So **c5 cannot take on c4** έΑΦ that would be a forward έΑεcapture,έΑζ which isnέΑβt allowed. The c-pawn is simply **blocked**; it has no legal moves in this position.

The same reasoning applies to **c5d4**: **d4** is empty, so a pawn cannot έΑεcaptureέΑζ there either.

## BlackέΑβs useful pieces

- **a7** is blocked by the white pawn on **a6**.
- **c5** is blocked by **c4**; no pawn moves for that pawn.
- So BlackέΑβs only moves come from the **king** and **bishop**.

## A solid reply

Player_Black (to Proxy):


Fetching legal moves from the Lichess cloud-eval API for this FEN:


HereέΑβs what went wrong, then a concrete move.

## Why `c5c4` is illegal

In chess, **pawns capture only on diagonals** (one step forward-diagonal), not straight ahead. On the c-file you have a **black pawn on c5** and a **white pawn on c4**. That is a **blocked pawn pair**: neither side can take the other έΑεhead-on.έΑζ The only way a pawn could capture on the c-file here would be **en passant**, but the FEN has **no** en passant square (`-`), so that does not apply.

So **`c5c4` is not a valid UCI move** έΑΦ itέΑβs neither a legal push (c4 is occupied) nor a legal capture (not diagonal).

## A strong legal move

The black bishop on **e5** can go to **f4**, giving **check** along the diagonal toward the white king on **c1** (path through empty squares on the long diagonal).

**Action:** `make_move e5f4`

Player_Black (to Proxy):


HereέΑβs what went wrong and what actually works.

## Why `c5c4` is illegal

From **c5**, a black pawn:

- **One step forward** is **c4**, but **c4** is occupied by a white pawn. Pawns may not capture straight ahead.
- **Captures** are only to **b4** and **d4**. On your FEN, those squares are empty, so the c-pawn has **no legal pawn moves** at all.

So `c5c4` is correctly rejected.

## Why `e5f4` was illegal

On **f4** the FEN has **`b`** έΑΦ a **black** bishop (`2P2b2` on rank 4). The king cannot take its own bishop.

## Legal Black moves (from your FEN)

White **c4** attacks **d5**, so the black king **cannot** go to **d5**.

**King (6):** `e5d6`, `e5d4`, `e5e4`, `e5e6`, `e5f5`, `e5f6`  

**Bishop (7):** `f4g5`, `f4h6`, `f4g3`, `f4h2`, `f4e3`, `f4d2`, `f4c1`  

**Pawns:** none (a7 and c5 are both stuck as above).

So you have **13** legal UCI moves in total.

## A concrete turn

A straightforward, always-legal choice is to slide the bishop:

```text
make_move f4g5
```

Other solid options: `make_move e5d4` (king toward the center), or `make_move f4h6` (bishop to the long diagonal).

If the host still rejects a move, paste the **exact** `get_legal_moves` output so we can match its formatting (some interfaces use promotion suffixes or different conventions).

Reasonable alternatives: **f4g5** or **f4h6** if you prefer to improve the bishop first; **e5e4** if you want the king one rank up immediately.

Player_Black (to Proxy):


HereέΑβs what went wrong and what to play instead.

## Why `c4c3` is illegal

In normal chess, **pawns never capture straight ahead**. A black pawn on **c4** moves toward **lower** ranks and takes only on **b3** and **d3**. The white king on **c3** is on the same file, so it is **not** a diagonal capture from **c4**. Advancing to **c3** would only be έΑεtakingέΑζ the king forward, which is not a legal pawn captureέΑΦso the UCI `c4c3` is correctly rejected.

So the c4 pawn is **blocked** by the king on c3 (it cannot go to c3, and there is nothing on b3/d3 to capture).

## A strong Black move

You want a **check** that uses the long diagonal through **c3**. From **d6**, **e5** sits on the **c3έΑΥd4έΑΥe5** diagonal, so:

**`make_move d6e5`** (bishop from d6 to e5 έΑΦ **Be5+**)

That gives check along the diagonal toward **c3**. After that, WhiteέΑβs king has to step off (e.g. toward **c2** / **d2**), and you can continue the attack.

If your interface supports it, **`get_legal_moves`** will list every legal UCI for Black in this FEN; **`d6e5`** should appear there.

---

**Action:** `make_move d6e5`

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Proxy (to Player_Black):

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Player_Black (to Proxy):


Black should keep the initiative with a forcing check. From **d6**, **e5** lies on the diagonal through **d4έΑΥc3**, so the bishop gives check: **Be5+**.

**Action:** `make_move d6e5`

After this, the white king cannot stay on **c3**; it must step to a safe square (for example **c2** or **d2**έΑΦyour engine will list what is legal), and Black can continue the king-and-pawn attack with the bishop cutting off escape squares.

Here is how the diagram reads (White at the bottom, rank 1 = bottom row):

- **White:** King on **e6**
- **Black:** King on **b5**, Queens on **c1** and **d2**
- **Side to move:** Black

So this is two black queens plus the black king against a lone white king έΑΦ a won position for Black.

A forcing, well-coordinated try is to bring the **d2** queen to the **6th rank** with check, eyeing the white king and keeping lines open for the **c1** queen:

**`make_move d2d6`**

That is **Qd2έΑΥd6+**, giving check along the d-file / toward **e6** and forcing the white king off the 6th rank. From there Black can continue with normal two-queen technique (drive the king to the edge and deliver mate). If you want a single alternative that also starts with a strong check from the same queen, **`d2h6`** (Qh6+) is another typical idea, but **`d2d6`** is an excellent practical choice here.
